danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat to player; capture results in game over

danger_signals.visual_form: Revision 0: Kevin scream pose; Revision 1: puff of smoke with 'oh no!' text
danger_signals.meaning: Game over condition; player has failed to evade bandits
Persistent threat; player must survive duration without capture

safety_signals.visual_form: Designated hideout spaces within level environment
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary refuge from enemy detection; strategic evasion points
Player agency in creating danger zones for enemies; obstacle mitigation

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Scream pose (Revision 0) vs. smoke/text (Revision 1) on capture
Two distinct enemy sprites with pursuit behavior
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Patrol patterns through level; capture-on-contact collision state

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Kevin sprite visible in level; enemies patrolling; hideouts and trap locations accessible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player can move freely, lay traps, enter hideouts; enemies pursue but haven't caught player

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Capture screen with Kevin scream (v0) or smoke/text 'oh no!' (v1); picture frames repositioned (v1)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game halts; level restarts from beginning; one-life mechanic enforces consequence

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Real-time 20-minute survival window; implicit through continuous gameplay pressure
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must maintain evasion throughout duration; no pause or reset available during countdown

environmental_storytelling.example: Designated safe zones within the house communicate to player which locations provide temporary refuge from bandits
Kevin's ability to lay various traps reinforces his ingenuity and establishes the house as a weapon in his defense strategy
Permanent failure on capture communicates high stakes and danger; loss of progress underscores threat severity

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Moves freely, enters hideouts, deploys traps; responsive to player input
Triggers game-over screen; displays scream or smoke reaction; returns to level start
Active on-screen; pursue player character; collision triggers capture state

entity_behavioral_tells.visual_form: Implied through trap mechanic; player can hinder their movement
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Can be temporarily blocked or slowed by deployed traps

notes: Home Alone NES communicates world-state and danger primarily through binary states: safe (evasion mode) vs. danger (capture). The game uses a real-time 20-minute survival window as its core pressure mechanism. Visual language is minimal due to NES hardware constraints, relying on sprite collision, hideout locations, and trap placement to convey strategic options. The two-revision capture screen (scream vs. smoke) represents developer refinement in communicating failure state. No faction color-coding or complex political systems exist; the conflict is purely binary (player vs. bandits). Environmental storytelling emerges through the house layout itself—hideouts and trap positions teach player where safety and strategy converge.
